FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 14, 2006

Jennifer K. McCain
Assistant City Attorney
City of Escondido
201 North Broadway
Escondido, CA 92025

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-06-031

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Councilmember Sam Abed, City of Escondido regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because we do not have information sufficient to fully answer your questions, we provide you with informal assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3).

QUESTION

May a city council member who owns income property in the Mercado District, which is within the Downtown Specific Plan Area (the "Area"), participate in: (1) workshops at which staff will ask the city council for direction on how to proceed with possible changes to the Area and which will be attended by members of the planning commission and the design review board; and (2) any future governmental decisions involving the Mercado District or the Area?

CONCLUSION

Because Councilmember Abed, the planning commission and the design review board do not appear to have a governmental decision pending before them, we can only provide general guidance as to whether the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions would preclude Councilmember Abed from participating in the workshops or in any future governmental decisions as a city council member. While Councilmember Abed clearly has economic interests in the Mercado District which is within the Area, the Act only

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. I-06-031
Page No. 2

bars his participation in the workshops or city council decisions if governmental
decisions to be made by the planning commission, the design review board or the city
council will have a material financial effect on his economic interests. If a decision
would have such an effect, he would be precluded from making, participating in making,
or using his official position to influence those particular governmental decisions.

We have identified three economic interests arising out of Councilmember Abed's
ownership of the Mercado District property. One is the property itself. Another is the
tenants who are sources of income to him. The third is his rental business. If it is
reasonably foreseeable that the planning commission or the design review board will
make governmental decision that have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on any of these interests, he may not participate in the workshops. Similarly, if it is
reasonably foreseeable that a decision by the city council will have a material financial
effect on these interests, Councilmember Abed should recuse himself.

FACTS

Councilmember Abed owns real property in the City of Escondido located at 540
and 562 W. Grand Avenue. Councilmember Abed receives income from one of the two
properties. Each of these properties is located in the Mercado District which is one of
eleven districts comprising the Area.

The city council is expected to participate in a downtown specific plan workshop
in mid-March. This workshop will involve a discussion between the city council, the
planning commission, the design review board, and local private sector members of the
downtown specific plan committee. At the workshop, city staff will be asking the city
council to direct them on how to proceed with possible changes to the Area. Items to be
discussed and decided may include the following: whether to reduce the number of
districts from eleven to seven, whether to proceed with a comprehensive environmental
impact review for the planning area, and whether changes to each district (including the
Mercado District) should be made, including changes to the permitted uses, density,
height, setbacks, parking, design, land use, build out density, infrastructure, timing, and
numerous other developmental standards. Any future changes to the Area would require
the city council to adopt changes to the Escondido zoning code.

The city council has not yet made any decisions regarding the Area or the
Mercado District. Councilmember Abed seeks our formal opinion as to whether he may
participate in the workshop and whether he may participate in any future governmental
decisions involving the Area and/or the Mercado District if his current ownership
interests remain the same. Councilmember Abed also seeks our formal opinion regarding
the procedures he must follow if he is required to abstain from participating in any
governmental decisions regarding the Area and/or the Mercado District.

File No. I-06-031
Page No. 3


ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.

The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
a public official has a disqualifying conflict-of-interest in a governmental decision.
(Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists
whenever a public official makes a governmental decision which has a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her financial interests.

While Councilmember Abed has asked guidance on two distinct questions (his
participation in the workshops and his making and participating in governmental
decisions as a city council member regarding the downtown specific plan area), the same
provisions of the Act's conflict-of-interest laws apply.

Step One. Is Councilmember Abed a "public official" within the meaning of
section 87100?

Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency." Councilmember Abed has correctly
identified himself as a public official as a member of the city council of Escondido which
is a local government agency.

Step Two. By participating in the workshops, would Councilmember Abed
be making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision? By
participating in future city council decisions involving the Downtown Specific Plan
Area and/or the Mercado District, would he be making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decision maker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision if, for the purpose of influencing the

File No. I-06-031
Page No. 4

decision, the official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or
consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)

You state that the workshops will be attended by members of the planning
commission, the design review board, city staff and others and that at these workshops
city staff will be asking the city council to direct them on how to proceed with possible
changes to the downtown specific plan areas. Without knowing the decision-making
powers, source of budget or manner of appointment of planning commissioners or
members of the design review board, we cannot say whether Councilmember Abed's
participation at the workshops would constitute participation in the making of
governmental decisions. However, it appears that by directing city staff at the workshops
he would be attempting to use his official position to influence a governmental decision
within the meaning of Regulation 18702.3(a):

"With regard to a governmental decision which is within or before an
official's agency or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of his or her agency, the official is attempting to use his or her
official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose of
influencing the decision, the official contacts, or appears before, or
otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee or
consultant of the agency."

Presumably, the city council has budgetary control over the planning commission
and the design review board and the commission and the board will be making
governmental decisions on matters discussed at the workshops. If so, Councilmember
Abed's participation in the workshops as you have described it, would amount to
influencing a governmental decision within the meaning of regulation 18702.3(a). We
reach this conclusion on the basis that, presumably, the commission and the board are
agencies "subject to the budgetary control" of the city council, which is Councilmember
Abed's "agency."2

As to Councilmember Abed's participation in future city council decisions
involving the Area, he would clearly be participating in a governmental decision.

If, after completing the eight-step analysis for a particular governmental decision,
Councilmember Abed concludes that he has a conflict-of-interest with respect
to a particular decision, then we would direct his attention to regulation 18702.5(b).3

2 If the commission or the board are not considered to be "within or before" the city council or
subject to the city council's budgetary control, then they are consider some other agency and Regulation
18702.3(b) would apply: "With regard to a governmental decision which is within or before an agency not
covered by subsection (a), the official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official acts or purports to act on behalf of, or as
the representative of, his or her agency to any member, officer, employee or consultant of an agency."

3 The regulation provides that if a public official who holds an offices specified in section 87200
has a conflict-of-interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must: (1) immediately prior to

File No. I-06-031
Page No. 5


Step Three. What are Councilmember Abed's economic interests? What are the possible sources of a financial conflict-of-interest?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts-of-interest arising from economic interests. These economic interests are described in sections 18703.1 through 18703.5 of the Commission's regulations. There are six kinds of economic interests from which conflicts-of-interest can arise:

1. Business Investment. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he, his spouse, or his registered domestic partner, or his dependent children or anyone acting on his behalf has invested $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a); regulation 18703.1(a).)

2. Business Employment or Management. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity for which he is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b).)

3. Real Property. A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he, his spouse, his registered domestic partner, or his dependent children or anyone acting on his behalf has invested $2,000 or more; or such person has a leasehold interest in real estate. (Section 87103(b); regulation 18703.2.)

4. Sources of Income. A public official has an economic interest in anyone, whether an individual or an organization, from whom he has received (or from whom he has been promised) $500 or more in income within 12 months prior to the decision about which he is concerned. (Section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3.)

5. Gifts. A public official has an economic interest in anyone, whether an individual or an organization, who has given him gifts which total $360 or more within 12 months prior to the decision about which he is concerned. (Regulation 18703.4).

6. Personal Financial Effect. A public official has an economic interest in his personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his immediate family. If these expenses, income, assets or liabilities are likely to go up or down as a result of the governmental decision, then the decision has a personal financial effect on the public official. (Regulation 18703.5.)

The information provided in your letter indicates that Councilmember

the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in sections 18702.5(c) and 18702.5(d) apply.

File No. 1-06-031
Page No. 6

Abed has four kinds of economic interests from which a conflict-of-interest may arise.

If Councilmember Abed has an ownership interest of $2,000 or more in each of the two properties located on W. Grand Avenue at the time that he makes, participates in making or uses his official position to influence a governmental decision, then under regulation 18703.2, he will have an economic interest in each of these properties.³

You state that Councilmember Abed receives rental income from the property located at 562 W. Grand Avenue. Under regulation 18703.3 he will have an economic interest in each tenant (a source of income) who has paid him $500 or more in rent during the 12 months preceding any governmental decision.

Under section 87103(a) and regulation 18703.1(a), Councilmember Abed will have an economic interest in his rental business (a business investment) if it is worth $2,000 or more, which presumably it is.

Finally, Councilmember Abed would have an economic interest in his personal finances if the income he derives from his rental property may go up or down as a result of a governmental decision in which he participates.

**Step Four. Assuming that the planning commission and the design review board will be acting as decision makers on matters discussed at the workshops, will Councilmember Abed's economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in these decisions? Will Councilmember Abed's economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in decisions he will make or participate in as a city council member?**

An economic interest which is directly involved in, and therefore directly affected by, a governmental decision creates a greater risk of a conflict-of-interest than does an economic interest which is only indirectly involved in the decision. Separate rules govern different kinds of economic interest for purposes of determining whether an economic interest is directly or indirectly involved.

**A. Real property.** The standard for determining direct or indirect involvement of Councilmember Abed's real property is found in regulation 18704.2(a)(1):

"The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the governmental decision…".

Because Councilmember Abed's property is located squarely within the Mercado

³ You inquire only about possible conflicts-of-interest arising due to Councilmember Abed's ownership of the two properties located on W. Grand Avenue. Thus, our response is limited to this context and does not consider possible conflicts-of-interest arising from other economic interests he may have.

File No. I-06-031
Page No. 7

District, his property will be directly affected by any governmental decision involving the
Mercado District. Because his property is also within the Area, his property will be
directly affected by any governmental decision involving the Area. (Regulation
18705.2(a)(1).)

B. Sources of Income and Business Investments . The standard for
determining direct or indirect involvement with respect to sources of income and business
investments is the same:

"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and
sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's
agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:

(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's
agency. A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to,
or contract with, the subject person." (Regulation 18704.1)

If none of the tenants (sources of income) who have paid Councilmember Abed
$500 or more during the 12 months preceding a governmental decision initiate the
proceeding or are named parties or are the subject of the proceeding, the tenants are
indirectly affected. If Councilmember Abed's rental business does not initiate a
proceeding nor is a named party or the subject of the proceeding, it too will be only
indirectly affected. The facts you present do not suggest that any particular member of
the community initiated the workshops. Accordingly, the tenants and the rental business
appear to be indirectly affected by the workshops.

If, however, future decisions that come before the planning commission, the
design review board or the city council are initiated by either tenants who have paid
Councilmember Abed $500 or more in the preceding 12 months, or the tenants are named
parties or the subject of the proceeding, then the tenants will be directly affected by the
decision made at that proceeding. Similarly, if Councilmember Abed's rental business
itself initiates a proceeding or is a named party or is the subject of the proceeding, then
his business will be directly affected by decisions made at that proceeding.

C. Personal Finances. Under regulation 18704.5 Councilmember Abed is
deemed to be directly involved in a governmental decision which has any financial effect
on his personal finances or those of his immediate family. Receipt of rental income from
his property falls within this provision.

File No. I-06-031
Page No. 8


Step Five. Applicable Materiality Standard

A. Real Property. Regulation 18705.2, (a)(1) provides that the financial effect of
a governmental decision on real property which is directly involved in the governmental
decision is presumed to be material. This presumption may be rebutted only by proof
that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any
financial effect on the real property –even a penny's worth. Without more information,
we could only speculate as to whether there are facts sufficient to rebut the presumption
of materiality in Councilmember Abed's situation. It is the duty of the public official to
determine the particular financial effects on his or her economic interests.

B. Sources of Income and Business Investments. Different standards to
determine materiality apply depending on the type of the source of income. Because we
have little information about Councilman Abed's tenants, we cannot determine which
standard to apply. We will therefore provide analyses for both individuals and business
entities.⁵ For purposes of this analysis, we will also assume that after applying Step Four,
Councilmember Abed would conclude that both his tenants and his rental business are
indirectly affected by the governmental decisions.

If the source of income (the tenant) is a business entity, the materiality standards
set forth in regulation 18705.1(c) vary according to whether the business entity is listed in
the Fortune 500, is listed on the New York Stock Exchange, is listed on the NASDAQ or
American Stock Exchange (or has income or before-tax earnings in an amount that would
make the business eligible for listing on the NASDAQ or American Stock Exchange) or
is not listed and is not eligible for listing. For purposes of this advice, we will assume
that the tenants are not listed in the Fortune 500, are not listed on any of the named stock
exchanges, and are not eligible for listing.⁶ Under this assumption, the indirect financial
effect of a governmental decision on the tenant is material only if it is reasonably
foreseeable that:

"(A) The government decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
$20,000 or more; or

(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or

___________________________
⁵ If it turns out that one of the tenants is a non-profit entity, we would direct your attention to
regulation 18705.3(b)(2).

⁶ If this assumption is incorrect, your attention is directed to Regulation 18705.1, subdivisions
(c)(1)-(3).

File No. I-06-031
Page No. 9

eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of
$5,000 or more; or

(C) The government decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or
liabilities of $20,000 or more." (Regulation 18705.1(c)(4).)

With respect to Councilmember Abed's rental business, we apply the same
analysis. Presumably the business is not listed on any exchange nor is it large enough to
qualify for listing. Accordingly, the indirect financial effect of a governmental decision
on this business will be material only if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
result in: an increase/decrease in gross revenues of $20,000 or more; the
incurring/avoidance of additional expenses or reduction/elimination of existing expenses
of $5,000 or more; or an increase/decrease in the value of the business assets/liabilities of
$20,000 or more.

For tenants who are individuals, the indirect financial effect of a governmental
decision is material if the decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or
other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property) by $1,000 or
more. (Reg. 18705.3(b)(3)(A).) If the tenant's assets or liabilities that are affected
consist of real property, then the indirect financial effect is presumed not to be material.7

C. Personal Finances. Under regulation 18705.5 a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on a public official's personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in
any 12-month period. However, there are exceptions relating to real property which
apply to Councilmember Abed's situation. Neither a financial effect on the value of real
property nor a financial effect on the gross revenues, expense, or value of assets and
liabilities of a business entity are considered in the materiality test for personal finances.
Receipt of rental income falls within the definition of "business entity."8 Hence,
Councilmember Abed's personal finances are deemed not to be affected by a
governmental decision that would increase the value of his property or would increase the
amount he receives in rental income from his rental business.

Step Six. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of a
governmental decision on Councilmember Abed's economic interests will meet the
applicable materiality standard? (Does a conflict-of-interest exist?)

7 This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the
governmental decisions, its financial effect, or the nature of the real property. The specific factors to
consider in determining whether such circumstances exist are beyond the scope of this letter. Your
attention is directed to regulation 18705.2(b).

8 Section 82005 defines "business entity" as any organization or enterprise operated for profit,
including but not limited to a proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture, syndicate,
corporation or association. Presumably, Councilmember Abed is collecting rent as part of a proprietorship
operated for profit.

File No. I-06-031
Page No. 10


An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Thorner, (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

A. Real Property. Regulation 18705.2(a)(1) provides that the financial effect of a
governmental decision on real property which is directly involved in the governmental
decision is presumed to be material. This presumption may be rebutted only by proof
that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any
financial effect on the real property –even a penny's worth.

Applying this standard to Councilmember Abed's Mercado District property, it is
presumed that any decisions of the planning commission, the design review board, or the
city council that will have a financial effect on his property will be material, unless it is
not reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have any effect on his property. Each
governmental decision is fact specific and so it is difficult to say under what
circumstances it is not reasonably foreseeable that a decision will have any effect on the
property. We have advised in the past that because specific plan decisions (one of the
potential decisions you have described) will almost inevitably designate specific land
uses, property within the specific plan area will almost inevitably have some impact on
the value of the property. (Busby Advice Letter, No. A-05-159.) We have also advised
that votes to conduct an environmental impact report are basic major policy decisions
requiring disqualification of public officials owning real property in the affected area.
(Eiser Advice Letter, No. A-93-237.)

B. Sources of Income. Because we lack information about Councilmember
Abed's tenants, we cannot advise whether any decisions of the planning commission, the
design review board, or the city council relating to the Mercado District or the Area
would have a material financial effect upon them.

C. Business Investment. Applying the materiality standard for business entities,
each decision must be evaluated to determine if it is foreseeable that the decision will
have an economic effect reaching the thresholds set forth in Regulation 18705.1(c)(4).
For example, given the facts, it is foreseeable that a city council decision to change
developmental standards (such as permitted uses, density, height, and setbacks) could
cause the value of Councilmember Abed's rental-business assets or gross revenues to
increase or decrease by $20,000 or more and he would have a conflict-of-interest with
respect to that decision.

Step Seven. The "public generally" exception.

Even if a public official determines that his economic interest will experience a
material financial effect as a result of the governmental decision, he may still participate

File No. I-06-031
Page No. 11

under the "public generally" exception if the material financial effect is not
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. (Section 87103; regulation 18707.)

Regulation 18707(b) sets forth a four-step process to determine if the effect of a
decision is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally:

"(1) Step One: Identify each specific person or real property
(economic interest) that is materially affected by the governmental
decision.

(2) Step Two: For each person or real property identified in Step
One, determine the applicable "significant segment" rule according
to the provisions of Regulation 18707.1(b).

(3) Step Three: Determine if the significant segment is affected by
the governmental decision as set forth in the applicable
"significant segment" rule. If the answer is "no", then the analysis
ends because the first prong of a two-part test set forth in
Regulation 18707.1(b) is not met, and the public official cannot
participate in the governmental decision. If the answer is "yes,"
proceed to Step Four.

(4) Step Four: Following the provisions of Regulation
18707.1(b)(2), determine if the person or real property identified in
Step One is affected by the governmental decision in "substantially
the same manner" as other persons or real property in the
applicable significant segment. If the answer is "yes" as to each
person or real property identified in Step One, then the effect of the
decision is not distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally and the public official may participate in the decision. If
the answer is "no" as to any person or real property identified in
Step One, the public official may not participate in the
governmental decision unless one of the special rules set forth in
Regulations 18707.2 through 18707.9 applies to each person or
real property triggering the conflict of interest." (Reg. 18707(b)(1-
4).)

Application of the Four-Pronged Test

Step One: Assuming that the presumption of materiality is not overcome by
rebuttal evidence as described in Step Six, Councilmember Abed's real property interests
will be materially affected by governmental decisions that could result from the
workshops and subsequent city council decisions, such as decisions that would change
permitted uses, density, height, setbacks, parking, design, land use, build out density,

File No. I-06-031
Page No. 12

infrastructure, timing and other developmental standards within the Mercado District or
the Area.

Presumably, Councilmember's rental business would also be materially affected
by such governmental decisions.³

Step Two: The applicable "significant segment rule" is different for interests in
real property, interests that affect a business entity and interests that affect an individual
who is a source of income to a public official.

Real Property. Under regulation 18707.1(b)(1)(B) a governmental decision will
affect a "significant segment" of the public generally if the decision also affects (i) ten
percent or more of all property owners or all homeowners in the jurisdiction of the
official's agency or the district the official represents; or (ii) 5,000 property owners or
homeowners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency.

Source of Income (Tenants). Under regulation 18707.1(b)(1)(A) a governmental
decision will affect a "significant segment" of the public generally if the decision also
affects: (i) ten percent or more of the population in the jurisdiction of the official's
agency or the district the official represents; or (ii) 5,000 individuals who are residents of
the jurisdiction.

Business Entity (Rental Business). For decisions that affect a business entity in
which a public official has an interest, the decision will affect a "significant segment" of
the public generally if it also affects either 2,000 or 25% of all business entities in the
jurisdiction or in the district the official represents. Regulation 18707.1(b)(1)(C).

Step Three: Under the facts presented, the significant segment for Councilman
Abed's real property interest may include: (i) all property owners in the Mercado
District; (ii) all property owners in the Area; or (iii) all property owners in the city itself,
depending on what decision is under consideration. For example, decisions relating to
density, land use and infrastructure could affect property owners outside the Area while
decisions relating to design, parking, height and setbacks would presumably only affect
property owners in the Mercado District or the Area, as the case may be.

Using the Mercado District as the significant segment, a decision that would
financially affect all property owners in the Mercado District would result in Councilman
Abed meeting the 10% threshold if such property owners make up 10% or more of all
property owners in the city, or 5,000 property owners. (This assumes that the decision
would have no affect on property owners outside the Mercado District.) However, he
would still need to consider all the various financial effects in determining whether a

³ We have insufficient information upon which to determine whether Councilmember Abed's
tenants (his sources of income) would be materially affected by these same decisions.

File No. I-06-031
Page No. 13

decision would financially affect all the property owners in the Mercado District in
"substantially the same manner" and apply the test set forth in step four.10

It may be helpful to examine an example of how to apply the test where a decision
would have a financial effect on all businesses within the entire city. In this example,
suppose the city council is voting on a decision to levy a flat fee upon all businesses
operating in the city to pay for infrastructure within the Area necessary to accommodate
higher density in the Area. In this example, the "public generally" exception would apply
only if the financial effect of the decision would not be distinguishable from its effect on
2,000 businesses or 25% of all businesses in the city.

If a governmental decision will affect an individual who is a source of income to
the council member (Councilmember Abed's tenants), the other test applies. For
example, suppose the city council will be voting on a decision to charge renters in the
Mercado District one-half of the usual charge for city-provided utilities. The decision
would affect a "significant segment" of the population generally if the renters in the
Mercado District, including Councilmember Abed's tenants, make up 10% or more of the
city's population or there are 5,000 such individuals.

Step Four: The final step requires the official to first identify what are the
material financial effects of a decision upon his economic interests. In this step,
Councilmember Abed would need to measure the financial effects upon his property, his
source of income (tenants) and his business entity (the rental business) against the
financial effects on other real property owners, other tenants and other businesses to
determine if the others are affected in "substantially the same manner" as his interest.

Without knowing what particular governmental decision will be considered, we
cannot identify the significant segments nor can we determine if Councilmember Abed's
economic interests will be financially affected in "substantially the same manner" as
others in the segments.

Step Eight: Even if Councilmember Abed has a disqualifying conflict of
interest, is his participation legally required?

In rare circumstances, an official may be called upon to take part in a decision
despite the fact that he or she has a disqualifying conflict-of-interest. This "legally

10 In the Doi Advice Letter, No. I-04-076, we stated that "[w]ith regard to determining the
financial effect of a decision, we have advised that, because the Commission is not a finder of fact, the
official must make a good faith effort to assess the effect of the decision by using some reasonable and
objective methods of valuation. (Moock Advice Letter, No. A-01-150.) Such a good faith effort may
require that additional information regarding the potential financial effect of the decision be gathered; a
public official is not required to but may choose to utilize professional services, such as those of a
professional apprais[er], to assist in assessing the financial effect of a decision for conflict-of-interest
purposes. (Ho Advice Letter, No. A-00-241.)"

File No. I-06-031
Page No. 14

required participation" rule applies in very specific circumstances where a government
agency would be unable to act without the official's participation. This is an exception
that typically applies when an agency is unable to assemble a quorum of its members
without the participation of an official with a conflict-of-interest.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Valentina Joyce

sections.conclusion:
Because Councilmember Abed, the planning commission and the design review
board do not appear to have a governmental decision pending before them, we can only
provide general guidance as to whether the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions would
preclude Councilmember Abed from participating in the workshops or in any future
governmental decisions as a city council member. While Councilmember Abed clearly
has economic interests in the Mercado District which is within the Area, the Act only
.rSi,r-q-8
bars his participation in the workshops or city council decisions if governmental
decisions to be made by the planning commission, the design review board or the city
council will have a material financial effect on his economic interests. If a decision
would have such an effect, he would be precluded from rnaking, participating in making,
or using his official position to influence those particular goveflrmental decisions.
We have identified three economic interests arising out of Councilmember Abed's
ownership of the Mercado District property. One is the property itself. Another is the
tenants who are sources of income to him. The third is his rental business. If it is
reasonably foreseeable that the planning commission or the design review board will
make governmental decision that have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on any of these interests, he may not participate in the workshops. Similarly, if it is
reasonably foreseeable that a decision by the city council will have amateial financial
effect on these interests, councilmember Abed should recuse himself.
sections.facts:
Councilmember Abed owns real property in the City of Escondido located at 540
and 562 W. Grand Avenue. Councilmember Abed receives income from one of the two
properties. Each of these properties is located in the Mercado District which is one of
eleven districts comprising the Area.
The city council is expected to participate in a downtown specific plan workshop
in mid-March. This workshop will involve a discussion between the city council, the
planning commission, the design review board, and local private sector members of the
downtown specific plan committee. At the workshop, city staff will be asking the city
council to direct them on how to proceed with possible changes to the Area. Items to be
discussed and decided may include the following: whether to reduce the number of
districts from eleven to seven, whether to proceed with a comprehensive environmental
impact review for the planning area, andwhether changes to each district (including the
Mercado District) should be made, including changes to the permitted uses, density,
height, setbacks, parking, design, land use, build out density, infrastructure, timing, and
numerous other developmental standards. Any future changes to the Area would require
the city council to adopt changes to the Escondido zoning code.
The city council has not yet made any decisions regarding the Area or the
Mercado District. Councilmember Abed seeks our formal opinion as to whether he may
participate in the workshop and whether he may participate in any future govemmental
decisions involving the Area and/or the Mercado District if his current ownership
interests remain the same. Councilmember Abed also seeks our formal opinion regarding
the procedures he must follow if he is required to abstain from participating in any
governmental decisions regarding the Area and/or the Mercado District.
sections.analysis:
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section S1001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
a public official has a disqualifying conflict-of-interest in a governmental decision.
(Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists
whenever a public official makes a govemmental decision which has a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her financial interests.
While Councilmember Abed has asked guidance on two distinct questions (his
participation in the workshops and his making and participating in govemmental
decisions as a city council member regarding the downtown specific plan area), the same
provisions of the Act's conflict-of-interest laws apply.
Step One. Is Councilmember Abed a 66public official" within the meaning of
section 87100?
Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency." Councilmember Abed has correctly
identified himself as a public official as a member of the city council of Escondido which
is a local goverlment agency.
Step Two. By participating in the workshops, would Councilmember Abed
be making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision? By
participating in future city council decisions involving the Downtown Specific plan
Area and/or the Mercado District, would he be making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.L) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decision maker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision if; for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or
consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)
You state that the workshops will be attended by rnembers of the planning
commission, the design review board, city staff and others and that at these workshops
city staff will be asking the city council to direct them on how to proceed with possible
changes to the downtown specific plan areas. Without knowing the decision-making
powers, source of budget or manner of appointment of planning commissioners or
members of the design review board, we cannot say whether Councilmember Abed's
participation at the workshops would constitute participation in the making of
governmental decisions. However, it appears that by directing city staff at the workshops
he would be attempting to use his official position to influence a governmental decision
within the meaning of Regulation 18702.3(a):
"With regard to a governmental decision which is within or before an
official's agency or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of his or her agency, the official is attempting to use his or her
official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose of
influencing the decision, the official contacts, or appears before, or
otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee or
consultant of the agency."
Presumably, the city council has budgetary control over the planning commission
and the design review board and the commission and the board will be making
governmental decisions on matters discussed at the workshops. If so, Councilmember
Abed's participation in the workshops as you have described it, would amount to
influencing a goverlmental decision within the meaning of regulationlSTO2.3(a). We
reach this conclusion on the basis that, presumably, the commission and the board are
agencies "subject _to the budgetary control" of the city council, which is Councilmember
Abed's "agency."2
As to Councilmember Abed's participation in future city council decisions
involving the Area, he would clearly be participating in a governmental decision.
If, after completing the eight-step analysis for a particular governmental decision,
Councilmember Abed concludes that he has a conflict-of-interest with respect
to a particular decision, then we would direct his attention to regulation 18702.5(b).3
2 If the commission or the board are not considered to be "within or before" the city council or
subject to the city council's budgetary control, then they are consider some other agency and Regulation
18702.3(b) would apply: "With regard to a governmental decision which is within or before an agency not
covered by subsection (a), the official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official acts or purports to act on behalf of, or as
the representative of, his or her agency to any member, officer, employee or consultant of an agency.,'
3 The regulation provides that if a public offrcial who holds an offices specified in section 87200
has a conflict-of-interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must: (l) immediately prior to
Step Three. What are Councilmember Abed's economic interests? What are
the possible sources of a financial conflict-of-interest?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts-of-interest arising
from economic interests. These economic interests are described in sections 18703.1
through 18703.5 of the Commission's regulations. There are six kinds of economic
interests from which conflicts-of-interest can arise:
1. Business Investment. A public official has an economic interest in a business
entity in which he, his spouse, or his registered domestic partner, or his dependent
children or anyone acting on his behalf has invested $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a);
regulation 1 8703. 1 (a).)
2. Business Emplo,-rment or Management. A public official has an economic
interest in a business entity for which he is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee,
or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b).)
3. Real Property. A public official has an economic interest in real property in
which he, his spouse, his registered domestic partner, or his dependent children or anyone
acting on his behalf has invested $2,000 or more; or such person has a leasehold interest
in real estate. (Section 87103(b); regulation 18703.2.)
4. Sources of Income. A public official has an economic interest in anyone,
whether an individual or an organization, from whom he has received (or from whom he
has been promised) $500 or more in income within 12 months prior to the decision about
which he is concerned. (Section 87103(c); regulation i8703.3.)
5. Gifts. A public officiai has an economic interest in anyone, whether an
individual or an organization, who has given him gifts which total $360 or more within
12 months prior to the decision about which he is concerned. (Regulation 18703.4).
6. Personal Financial Effect. A public official has an economic interest in his
personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his immediate family
If these expenses, income, assets or liabilities are likely to go up or down as a result of
the governmental decision, then the decision has a personal financial effect on the public
official. (Regulation 18703.5.)
The information provided in your letter indicates that Counciknember
the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well
as details of the economic interest on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave
the room for the duration of the discussion and./or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars,
absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in
sections 18702.5(c) and 18702.5(d) apply.
Abed has four kinds of economic interests from which a conflict-of-interest may arise.
If Councilmember Abed has an ownership interest of $2,000 or more in each of
the two properties located on W. Grand Avenue at the time that he makes, participates in
making or uses his official position to influence a governmental decision, then under
regulation 18703.2, he will have an economic interest in each of these properties.a
You state that Councilmember Abed receives rental income from the property
located at 562 W. Grand Avenue. Under regulation 18703.3 he will have an economic
interest in each tenant (a source of income) who has paid him $500 or more in rent during
the 12 months preceding any govemmental decision.
Under section 87103(a) and regulation 18703.1(a), Councilmember Abed will
have an economic interest in his rental business (a business investment) if it is worth
$2,000 or more, which presumably it is.
Finally, Councilmember Abed would have an economic interest in his personal
finances if the income he derives from his rental property may go up or down as a result
of a governmental decision in which he participates.
Step Four. Assuming that the planning commission and the design review
board will be acting as decision makers on matters discussed at the workshops, will
Councilmember Abed's economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in these
decisions? Will Councilmember Abed's economic interests be directly or indirectly
involved in decisions he will make or participate in as a city council member?
An economic interest which is directly involved in, and therefore directly affected
by, a governmental decision creates a greater risk of a conflict-of-interest than does an
economic interest which is only indirectly involved in the decision. Separate rules
govern different kinds of economic interest for purposes of determining whether an
economic interest is directly or indirectly involved.
A. Real property. The standard for determining direct or indirect involvement of
councilmember Abed's real property is found in regulation 18704.2(a)(l):
"The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part
of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries
(or the proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject
of the governmental decision...".
Because Councilmember Abed's property is located squarely within the Mercado
a You inquire only about possible conflicts-of-interest arising due to Councilmember Abed's
ownership of the two properties located on W. Grand Avenue. Thus, our response is limited to this context
and does not consider possible conflicts-of-interest arising from other economic interests he may have.
District, his property will be directly affected by any govemmental decision involving the
Mercado District. Because his property is also within the Area, his property will be
directly affected by any govemmental decision involving the Area. (Regulation
1870s.2(a)(r).)
B. Sources of Income and Business Investments The standard for
determining direct or indirect involvement with respect to sources of income and business
investments is the same:
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and
sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's
agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:
(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's
agency. A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to,
or contract with, the subject person." (Regulation 18704.1)
If none of the tenants (sources of income) who have paid Councilmember Abed
$500 or more duringthe 12 months preceding a govemmental decision tnitiate the
proceeding or are named parties or are the subject of the proceeding, the tenants are
indirectly affected. If Councilmember Abed's rental business does not initiate a
proceeding nor is a named party or the subject of the proceeding, it too will be only
indirectly affected. The facts you present do not suggest that any particular member of
the community initiated the workshops. Accordingly, the tenants and the rental business
appear to be indirectly affected by the workshops.
If, however, future decisions that come before the planning commission, the
design review board or the city council are initiated by either tenants who have paid
Councilmember Abed $500 or more in the preceding 12 months, or the tenants are named
parties or the subject of the proceeding, then the tenants will be directly affected by the
decision made at that proceeding. Similarly, if Councilmember Abed's rental business
itself initiates a proceeding or is a named party or is the subject of the proceeding, then
his business will be directly affected by decisions made at that proceeding.
C. Personal Finances. Under regulation 18704.5 Councilmember Abed is
deemed to be directly involved in a governmental decision which has any financial effect
on his personal finances or those of his immediate family. Receipt of rental income from
his property falls within this provision.
Step Five. Applicable Materiality Standard
A. Real Property. Regulation 18705.2, (a)(1) provides that the financial effect of
a govemmental decision on real property which is directly involved in the govemmental
decision is presumed to be material. This presumption may be rebutted only by proof
that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision willhave any
financial effect on the real property -even a penny's worth. Without more information,
we could only speculate as to whether there are facts sufficient to rebut the presumption
of materiality in Councilmember Abed's situation. It is the duty of the public official to
determine the particular financial effects on his or her economic interests.
B. Sources of Income and Business Investments. Different standards to
determine materiality apply depending on the type of the source of income. Because we
have so little information about Councilman Abed's tenants, we cannot determine which
standard_ to apply. We will therefore provide analyses for both individuals and business
entities.5 For purposes of this analysii, we will also assume that after applying Step Four,
Councilmember Abed would conclude that both his tenants and his rental business are
indirectly affected by the govemmental decisions.
If the source of income (the tenant) is a business entity, the materiality standards
set forth in regulation 18705.1(c) vary according to whether the business entity is listed in
the Fortune 500, is listed on the New York Stock Exchange, is listed on the NASDAe or
American Stock Exchange (or has income or before-tax eamings in an amount that would
make the business eligible for listing on the NASDAQ or American Stock Exchange) or
is not listed and is not eligible for listing. For purposes of this advice, we will assume
that the tenants are not listed in the Fortune 500, are not listed on any of the named stock
exchanges, and are not eligible for listing.6 Under this assumption, ihe indirect financial
effect of a governmental decision on the tenant is material only if it is reasonably
foreseeable that:
"(A) The govemment decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
$20,000 or more; or
(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or
5 If it turns out that one of the tenants is a non-profit entity, we would direct your attention to
regulation 1 8705.3(b)(2).
u If this assumption is incorrect, your attention is directed to Regulation 18705.1, subdivisions
(c)( I )-(3 ).
eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of
$5,000 or more; or
(C) The government decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or
liabilities of $20,000 or more." (Regulation 18705.1(c)(a).)
with respect to councilmember Abed's rental business, we apply the same
analysis. Presumably the business is not listed on any exchange nor is it large enough to
qualify for listing. Accordingly, the indirect financial effect of a governmental decision
on this business will be material only if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
result in: an increase/decrease in gross revenues of $20,000 or more; or the
incurring/avoidance of additional expenses or reduction/elimination of existing expenses
of $5,000 or more; or an increase/decrease in the value of the business assets/liabilities of
S20,000 or more.
For tenants who are individuals, the indirect financial effect of a governmental
decision is material if the decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or
other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property) by $ 1,000 or
more. (Reg. 18705.3(b)(3XA).) If the tenant's assets or liabilities that are affected
consist of real property, then the indirect financial effect is presumed not to be material.T
C. Personal Finances. Under regulation 18705.5 a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on a public official's personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in
any 72-month period. However, there are exceptions relating to real property which
apply to Councilmember Abed's situation. Neither a financial effect on the value of real
property nor a financial effect on the gross revenues, expense, or value ofassets and
liabilities of a business entity are considered in the materiality test for personal finances.
Receipt of rental income falls within the definition of "business entity."8 Hence,
Councilmember Abed's personal finances are deemed not to be affected by a
governmental decision that would increase the value of his property or would increase the
amount he receives in rental income from his rental business.
Step Six. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of a
governmental decision on Councilmember Abed's economic interests will meet the
applicable materiality standard? (Does a conflict-of-interest exist?)
' This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the
governmental decisions, its financial effect, or the nafure of the real properfy. The specific factors to
consider in determining whether such circumstances exist are beyond the scope of this letter. your
attention is directed to regulation 18705.2(b).
8 Section 82005 def,rnes "business entity" as any organization or enterprise operated for profit,
including but not limited to a proprietorship, parbrership, firm, business kust, joint verlture, syndicate,
corporation or association. Presumably, Councilmember Abed is collecting rent as part of a proprietorship
operated for profit.
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Thorner, (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
A. Real Property. Regulation 18705.2(a)(1) provides that the financial effect of a
goverlmental decision on real property which is directly involved in the governmental
decision is presumed to be material. This presumption may be rebutted only by proof
that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision willhave any
financial effect on the real property -even a penny's worth.
Applying this standard to Councilmember Abed's Mercado District property, it is
presumed that any decisions of the planning commission, the design review board, or the
city council that will have a financial effect on his property will be material, unless it is
not reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have any effect on his property. Each
goverlmental decision is fact specific and so it is difficult to say under what
circumstances it is not reasonably foreseeable that a decision will have any effect on the
property. We have advised in the past that because specific plan decisions (one of the
potential decisions you have described) will almost inevitably designate specific land
uses, property within the specific plan area will almost inevitably have some impact on
the value of the property. (Busby Advice Letter, No. A-05-159.) We have also advised
that votes to conduct an environmental impact report are basic major policy decisions
requiring disqualification of public officials owning real property in the affected area.
(Eiser Advice Letter, No. A-93-237.)
B. Sources of Income. Because we lack information about Councilmember
Abed's tenants, we cannot advise whether any decisions of the planning commission, the
design review board, or the city council relating to the Mercado District or the Area
would have a material financial effect upon them.
C. Business Investment. Applyng the materiality standard for business entities,
each decision must be evaluated to determine if it is foreseeable that the decision will
have an economic effect reaching the thresholds set forth in Regulation 13705.1(c)(a).
For example, given the facts, it is foreseeable that a city council decision to change
developmental standards (such as permitted uses, density, height, and setbacks) could
cause the value of Councilmember Abed's rental-business assets or gross revenues to
increase or decrease by $20,000 or more and he would have a conflict-of-interest with
respect to that decision.
Step Seven. The "public generally,' exception.
Even if a public official determines that his econornic interest will experience a
material financial effect as a result of the govemmental decision, he may still participate
under the'public generally" exception if the material financial effect is not
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. (Section 87103; regulation 18707.)
Regulation 18707(b) sets forth a four-step process to determine if the effect of a
decision is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally:
"(1) Step One: Identify each specific person or real property
(economic interest) that is materially affected by the govemmental
decision.
(2) Step Two: For each person or real property identified in Step
One, determine the applicable "significant segment" rule according
to the provisions of Regulation 18707.1(b).
(3) Step Three: Determine if the significant segment is affected by
the govemmental decision as set forth in the applicable
"significant segment" rule. If the answer is "no," then the analysis
ends because the first prong of a two-part test set forth in
Regulation 18707.1(b) is not met, and the public official cannot
participate in the govemmental decision. If the answer is "yes,',
proceed to Step Four.
(4) Step Four: Following the provisions of Regulation
18707.1(b)(2), determine if the person or real property identified in
Step one is affected by the governmental decision in "substantiary
the same manner" as other persons or real property in the
applicable significant segment. If the answer is "yes" as to each
person or real property identified in Step One, then the effect of the
decision is not distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally and the public official may participate in the decision. If
the answer is "no" as to any person or real property identified in
Step One, the public official may not participate in the
governmental decision unless one of the special rules set forth in
Regulations 1.8707 .2 through 18707 .9 applies to each person or
real property triggering the conflict of interest." (Reg. 18707(bX1-
4).)
Aoolication of the Four-Pronsed Test
Step One: Assuming that the presumption of materiality is not overcome by
rebuttal evidence as described in Step Six, Councilmember Abed's real property interests
will be materially affected by governmental decisions that could result from the
workshops and subsequent city council decisions, such as decisions that would change
permitted uses, density, height, setbacks, parking, design, land use, build out density,
infrastructure, timing and other developmental standards within the Mercado District or
the Area.
Presumably, Councilmember's rental business would also be materially affected
by such governmental decisions.e
Step Two: The applicable "significant segment rule" is different for interests in
real property, interests that affect a business entity and interests that affect an individual
who is a source of income to a public official.
Real Property. Under regulation 18707.1(bxlXB) a goverrrmental decision will
affect a "significant segment" of the public generally if the decision also affects (i) ten
percent or more of all property owners or all homeowners in the jurisdiction of the
official's agency or the district the official represents; or (ii) 5,000 property owners or
homeowners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency.
Source of Income (Tenants). Under regulation 18707.1O)(1XA) a govemmental
decision will affect a "significant segment" of the public generally if the decision also
affects: (i) ten percent or more of the population in the jurisdiction of the official's
agerLcy or the district the official represents; or (ii) 5,000 individuals who are residents of
the jurisdiction.
Business Entity (Rental Business). For decisions that affect a business entity in
which a public official has an interest, the decision will affect a "significant segment" of
the public generally if it also affects either 2,000 or 25Yo of all business entities in the
jurisdiction or in the district the official represents. Regulation 18707.1(b)(1)(C).
Step Three: Under the facts presented, the significant segment for Councilman
Abed's real property interest may include: (i) all property owners in the Mercado
District; (ii) all property owners in the Area; or (iii) all property owners in the city itself,
depending on what decision is under consideration. For example, decisions relating to
density, land use and infrastructure could affect property owners outside the Area, while
decisions relating to design, parking, height and setbacks would presumably only affect
property owners in the Mercado District or the Area, as the case may be.
Using the Mercado District as the significant segment, a decision that would
financially affect all property owners in the Mercado District would result in Councilman
Abed meeting the 10o/o threshold if such property owners rnake up l\oh or more of all
property owners in the city, or 5,000 property owners. (This assumes that the decision
would have no affect on property owners outside the Mercado District.) However, he
would still need to consider all the various financial effects in determining whether a
e We have insufficient information upon which to determine whether Councilmember Abed's
tenants (his sources of income) would be materially affected by these same decisions.
decision would financially affect all the property owners in the Mercado District in
"substantially the same malrner" and apply the test set forth in step four.l0
It may be helpful to examine an example of how to apply the test where a decision
would have a financial effect on all businesses within the entire city. In this example,
suppose the city council is voting on a decision to levy a flat fee upon all businesses
operating in the city to pay for infrastructure within the Area necessary to accommodate
higher density in the Area. In this example, the "public generally" exception would apply
only if the financial effect of the decision would not be distinguishable from its effect on
2,000 businesses or 25o/o of all businesses in the city.
If a governmental decision will affect an individual who is a source of income to
the council member (Councilmember Abed's tenants), the other test applies. For
example, suppose the city council will be voting on a decision to charge renters in the
Mercado District one-half of the usual charge for city-provided utilities. The decision
would affect a "significant segment" of the population generally if the renters in the
Mercado District, including Councilmember Abed's tenants, make up l0o/o or more of the
city's population or there are 5,000 such individuals.
Step Four: The final step requires the official to first identify what are the
material financial effects of a decision upon his economic interests. In this step,
Councilmember Abed would need to measure the financial effects upon his property, his
source of income (tenants) and his business entity (the rental business) against the
financial effects on other real property owners, other tenants and other businesses to
determine if the others are affected in "substantially the same manner" as his interests.
Without knowing what particular govemmental decision will be considered, we
cannot identify the significant segments nor can we determine if Councilmember Abed's
economic interests will be financially affected in "substantially the same manner" as
others in the segments.
Step Eight: Even if Councilmember Abed has a disqualifying conflict of
interest, is his participation legally required?
In rare circumstances, an official may be called upon to take part in a decision
despite the fact that he or she has a disqualifying conflict-of-interest. This "legally
r0 In the Doi Advice Letter, No. I-04-076, we stated that "[w]ith regard to determining the
financial effect of a decision, we have advised that, because the Commission is not a finder of fact, the
official must make a good faith effort to assess the effect of the decision by using some reasonable and
objective methods of valuation. (Moock Advice Letter, No. 4'-01-150.) Such a good faith effort may
require that additional information regarding the potential financial effect of the decision be gathered; a
public official is not required to but may choose to utilize professional services, such as those ofa
professional apprais[er], to assist in assessing the financial effect ofa decision for conflict-of-interest
purposes. (Ho Advice Letter, No. ,4.-00-241.)"
required participation" rule applies in very specific circumstances where a government
agency would be unable to act without the official's participation. This is an exception
that typically applies when an agency is unable to assemble a quorum of its members
without the participation of an official with a conflict-of-interest.

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 April 14, 2006 Jennifer K. McCain Assistant City Attorney City of Escondido 201 North Broadway Escondido, CA 92025 RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-06-031 This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Councilmember Sam Abed, City of Escondido regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because we do not have information sufficient to fully answer your questions, we provide you with informal assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3). QUESTION May a city council member who owns income property in the Mercado District, which is within the Downtown Specific Plan Area (the "Area"), participate in: (1) workshops at which staff will ask the city council for direction on how to proceed with possible changes to the Area and which will be attended by members of the planning commission and the design review board; and (2) any future governmental decisions involving the Mercado District or the Area? CONCLUSION Because Councilmember Abed, the planning commission and the design review board do not appear to have a governmental decision pending before them, we can only provide general guidance as to whether the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions would preclude Councilmember Abed from participating in the workshops or in any future governmental decisions as a city council member. While Councilmember Abed clearly has economic interests in the Mercado District which is within the Area, the Act only ¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. I-06-031 Page No. 2 bars his participation in the workshops or city council decisions if governmental decisions to be made by the planning commission, the design review board or the city council will have a material financial effect on his economic interests. If a decision would have such an effect, he would be precluded from making, participating in making, or using his official position to influence those particular governmental decisions. We have identified three economic interests arising out of Councilmember Abed's ownership of the Mercado District property. One is the property itself. Another is the tenants who are sources of income to him. The third is his rental business. If it is reasonably foreseeable that the planning commission or the design review board will make governmental decision that have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of these interests, he may not participate in the workshops. Similarly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision by the city council will have a material financial effect on these interests, Councilmember Abed should recuse himself.
